claim: Entrepreneur and business strategist Daniel Priestley, in a March 2026 appearance on The Diary of a CEO with Steven Bartlett, predicted a major financial meltdown in 2029 driven by AI data-center capital expenditure. Priestley argued: that historically, when infrastructure spending exceeds 3 percent of GDP it has been followed by a roughly decade-long bust (citing UK and US railway booms, electrification, and highway buildouts); that current AI infrastructure spending is running at approximately $650 billion per year globally; that data-center hardware — GPUs and compute stacks — has a useful life of 3–4 years, versus 100 years for railroads, 50+ for highways, and 30 for fiber-optic networks; and that the user-side monetization gap, in which a small minority of AI users pay for premium services, makes the current capex trajectory financially unsustainable. He explicitly framed 2029 as the centennial of the Great Depression.

sources.label: The Diary of a CEO with Steven Bartlett — “Daniel Priestley: Plumbers Will Earn More Than Lawyers! I Predicted 2008, Now I'm Warning About 2029” (Mar 2026)
SingjuPost — full transcript of the Priestley episode
sources.quote: the financial model just makes no sense whatsoever at the moment

sources.label: Future AI / @future.is.ai, Instagram reel DWxV_gPD_DH (Apr 5, 2026) — surfacing source

notes: Priestley publicly stated he had also predicted the 2008 crash; that prior call is part of the episode's promotional framing. The historical pattern (infrastructure capex above 3 percent of GDP followed by a downturn) is the speaker's analytical claim, supported by his named examples rather than by a quantitative academic citation.
